answer:
**The incident is described in *Traité de Législation: Volume IV* by Charles Comte, Chapter 18 ("De l’influence réciproque de l’esclavage sur la religion, et de la religion sur l’esclavage").**[^1][^2]

This chapter recounts events in Barbados (specifically Bridgetown) in October 1823. Slave-owners formed a "secret committee," published a proclamation summoning "the gentry and other inhabitants" equipped with "the necessary tools" to demolish a Methodist chapel on the following Sunday, and proceeded over two days: they broke doors/windows, destroyed pews/pulpit/Bibles/other books, partially demolished the building, wrecked the missionary's home (furniture, roof, linen into flags), issued "three times three" victory howls, reconvened the next day to raze the chapel completely, then issued a second proclamation dated from Bridgetown on 21 October 1823.[^1][^2]

The requested proclamation is quoted as follows:

> Bridgetown, Wednesday, 21 October 1823.
>
> "The inhabitants of this island are respectfully informed that in consequence of the unprovoked and unmerited attacks, which have been repeatedly made by the community of Methodist missionaries, the villainous African Society (*otherwise known as agents to the villanous African Society*) [357], a number of *respectable gentlemen* have formed the resolution to put an end to the Methodist affair; that, with this in view, they began their work on Sunday evening, and that they have the very great satisfaction of announcing that **at midnight they completed the ruin of the church**. They must add to this information that the missionary escaped in a small vessel yesterday at noon, and has taken refuge on the island of St. Vincent, thereby avoiding the manifestation, toward him, of the public sentiments he so well deserved. It is to be hoped that, as this proclamation will be spread throughout all the islands and colonies, all persons who consider themselves *true friends of religion* will follow the laudable example of the Barbadians, by putting an end to Methodism and to Methodist churches."[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, Chapter 18 ("De l’influence réciproque de l’esclavage sur la religion, et de la religion sur l’esclavage"), [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/18-de-l-influence-reciproque-de-l-esclavage-sur-la-re/translation/#p-5549b4).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, Chapter 18, [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/18-de-l-influence-reciproque-de-l-esclavage-sur-la-re/translation/](works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/18-de-l-influence-reciproque-de-l-esclavage-sur-la-re/translation/).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, Chapter 18, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/18-de-l-influence-reciproque-de-l-esclavage-sur-la-re/translation/#p-641117).

"three times three" mob OR chapel OR church
